items.q: Which mood follows 'Espero que' (I hope that)?
items.why: Students use indicative for what feels certain. Expressions of hope, wish, doubt, emotion + QUE trigger SUBJUNCTIVE: Espero que..., Quiero que..., Dudo que..., Es importante que... Common in future plans/aspirations writing.

items.q: Where do object pronouns go in 'I see him' (lo veo)?
items.why: Students apply English word order. Spanish places object pronouns BEFORE conjugated verbs. They can attach to infinitives, gerunds, and positive commands - but not after a conjugated verb in normal sentences.

items.q: Why does 'Me gusta el chocolate' literally translate as 'Chocolate is pleasing to me'?
items.correct: Gustar works backwards - the thing liked is the subject, the person is the indirect object.
items.misconception: Gustar means 'to like' and works exactly like English.
items.random_wrong: It is a typo for 'Yo gusto'.
items.why: Students translate gustar as 'to like' with English structure. Actually gustar = 'to be pleasing to'. So 'me gustan los libros' (books are pleasing to me) - the verb agrees with the thing, not the liker.

items.q: Which pronoun is used in Spain but rare in LatAm?
items.why: It's Vosotros. “Ustedes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Plurilinguismo. “Monolinguismo” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate number of Spanish native speakers worldwide (millions)?
items.why: The real figure is 480. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
